items.title: Theker just raised $85M to build the factory robot that doesn’t specialize in anything
items.raw_content: Unlike humanoid robots designed around a fixed form — think Boston Dynamics — Theker's machines are built to be reconfigured.

items.title: Amazon’s data centers used 2.5 billion gallons of water last year
items.raw_content: Just after Seattle enacted a one-year data center moratorium that some of Amazon's own employees pushed for, Amazon shared how much water its data centers use, reportedly for the first time. items.title: Claude Fable won’t answer basic biology questions
items.raw_content: Anthropic just released Claude Fable 5, calling it the most powerful AI model it has ever made widely available and praising its skills in biology, among others. items.title: Jeff Bezos’ AI startup aims to build an ‘artificial general engineer’

items.title: Pool’s new app turns your screenshots into something useful
items.raw_content: Pool's new app automatically sorts screenshots into personalized collections, tracks down the original links behind saved content, and helps you rediscover products, recipes, travel ideas, and other things you meant to revisit.

items.title: Anthropic apologizes for invisible Claude Fable guardrails
items.raw_content: Anthropic has apologized for stealthily throttling its new AI model, Claude Fable 5, with hidden guardrails that undermine both researchers and rivals using it to develop competing systems. The company says it is reversing course and will be more transparent about when the restrictions kick in, even if that means Fable refuses more queries. Fable […]

items.title: Deezer launches an AI music detector for other streaming services
items.raw_content: Deezer will now scan your playlists on other streaming platforms to detect AI-generated music. Deezer was the first of the big streaming services to start labeling AI-generated music. It even offered its tech to other platforms, but it doesn't seem like it had many buyers. Qobuz launched its own detection tech, while Apple and Spotify […]

items.title: Google won’t just admit it’s feeding YouTube creators to its music AI
items.raw_content: If you've uploaded a song to YouTube, Google almost certainly considers your video fair game for training its Lyria music AI, it just won't admit it right now. A group of independent musicians is suing Google, claiming that it illegally used songs they uploaded to YouTube to train its Lyria 3 model. Google has filed […]
